title: Runway Calculator
summary: Calculate your runway, understand what drives it, and build a plan to extend it before you run out of time.
whenToUseIt: Monthly as part of your financial review, before major spending decisions, and whenever revenue or costs change significantly.

steps.title: Calculate your current monthly burn
steps.description: Total all monthly expenses: salaries, contractors, rent, software, hosting, marketing, insurance, everything. Then subtract monthly revenue. Net burn = total expenses - total revenue.

steps.title: Count your total cash
steps.description: Add up all available cash: bank accounts, savings, available credit lines you would actually draw on, committed receivables within 30 days. Do not count: investment commitments not yet received, projected revenue.

steps.title: Calculate runway
steps.description: Runway (months) = Total cash / Monthly net burn. Under 3 months = emergency. 3-6 months = urgent. 6-12 months = manageable. 12+ months = comfortable.

steps.title: Model three scenarios
steps.description: Calculate runway under: Worst case (revenue drops 20%), Base case (current trajectory), Best case (revenue grows 20%).
steps.aiPrompt: My monthly revenue is $[X], monthly expenses are $[Y], and I have $[Z] cash. Calculate my runway under 3 scenarios: 1) Revenue drops 20%, 2) Current trajectory, 3) Revenue grows 20%. For each, show monthly burn, runway in months, and recommended actions.

steps.title: Identify runway extension levers
steps.description: List every option: reduce headcount, cut discretionary spending, renegotiate contracts, accelerate revenue, raise prices, raise capital, defer hires. Prioritise by impact and reversibility.

expectedOutcome: A clear runway number, three scenarios modelled, and a prioritised list of levers for extending runway if needed.
commonMistakes: Using revenue projections instead of actual revenue
Forgetting one-off expenses (tax bills, annual subscriptions) that spike certain months
Not modelling scenarios — a single runway number gives false precision
Waiting until runway is under 3 months to act — that is too late
